question:
From the following, how many compounds contain at least one secondary alcohol?

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
A secondary alcohol has the OH group on a carbon bonded to exactly two other carbon atoms. Analyzing each structure:
I contains only primary alcohols and carboxylic acid.
II has OH on a carbon bonded to two other carbons (secondary alcohol).
III has tertiary and primary alcohols only.
IV has secondary alcohols on the cyclopentanone ring.
V has OH on a secondary carbon (between two CH groups).
VI has the central carbon as tertiary with three OH groups.
Compounds II, IV, and V contain at least one secondary alcohol, giving total = 3.

q:
The unsaturated ether on acidic hydrolysis produces carbonyl compounds as shown below:-
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}- & \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \\ & \downarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{(+)} \\ \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}= & \mathrm{O} \text { and } \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \end{aligned}$
Based on this, predict the solution/reagent that will help to distinguish " P " and " Q " obtained in the following reaction:-
question:
The unsaturated ether on acidic hydrolysis produces carbonyl compounds as shown below:-
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}- & \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \\ & \downarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{(+)} \\ \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}= & \mathrm{O} \text { and } \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \end{aligned}$
Based on this, predict the solution/reagent that will help to distinguish " P " and " Q " obtained in the following reaction:-
solution:
Acidic hydrolysis of vinyl ethers involves the addition of water across the double bond followed by the elimination of alcohol/phenol to form carbonyl compounds. In the given reaction:
$CH_3-CH=CH-O-C(CH_3)=CH_2 \xrightarrow{H_3O^+} P + Q$
The hydrolysis occurs at both vinyl ether linkages.
The left part $CH_3-CH=CH-O-$ hydrolyzes to form propanal: $CH_3-CH_2-CHO$ (P).
The right part $-O-C(CH_3)=CH_2$ hydrolyzes to form acetone: $CH_3-CO-CH_3$ (Q).
P is an aldehyde (propanal) and Q is a ketone (acetone).
Fehling solution is used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones. Aldehydes (except benzaldehyde) reduce Fehling solution to give a red precipitate of $Cu_2O$, while ketones do not react.
Lucas reagent is used for alcohols. 2,4-DNP reacts with both aldehydes and ketones. Saturated $NaHSO_3$ reacts with most aldehydes and methyl ketones, so it is not the best choice for distinguishing these specific compounds compared to Fehling solution.

q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Cross aldol condensation between two different aldehydes will always produce four different products.
Statement II: When semicarbazide reacts with a mixture of benzaldehyde and acetophenone under optimum pH, it forms a condensation product with acetophenone only.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Cross aldol condensation between two different aldehydes will always produce four different products.
Statement II: When semicarbazide reacts with a mixture of benzaldehyde and acetophenone under optimum pH, it forms a condensation product with acetophenone only.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
Statement I: Cross aldol condensation between two different aldehydes produces four products only if both aldehydes have $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms. If one aldehyde lacks $\alpha$-hydrogen (e.g., benzaldehyde and acetaldehyde), only two products are typically formed (one self and one cross). If neither has $\alpha$-hydrogen, no aldol condensation occurs. Thus, it does not 'always' produce four products. Statement I is false.

Statement II: Benzaldehyde is an aldehyde and acetophenone is a ketone. Aldehydes are generally more reactive than ketones towards nucleophilic addition reactions due to less steric hindrance and greater electronic deficiency of the carbonyl carbon. Therefore, semicarbazide will react faster with benzaldehyde than with acetophenone. The statement claiming it forms a product with acetophenone only is incorrect. Statement II is false.

Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The dipole moment of R-CN is greater than R-NC and R-NC can undergo hydrolysis under acidic medium to produce
: R-CN hydrolyses under acidic medium to produce a compound which on treatment with $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$, followed by the addition of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ gives another compound $(\mathrm{x})$. This compound $(\mathrm{x})$ on treatment with $\mathrm{NaOCl} / \mathrm{NaOH}$ gives a product, that on treatment with $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3} / \mathrm{KOH} / \Delta$ produces R-NC.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The dipole moment of R-CN is greater than R-NC and R-NC can undergo hydrolysis under acidic medium to produce
: R-CN hydrolyses under acidic medium to produce a compound which on treatment with $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$, followed by the addition of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ gives another compound $(\mathrm{x})$. This compound $(\mathrm{x})$ on treatment with $\mathrm{NaOCl} / \mathrm{NaOH}$ gives a product, that on treatment with $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3} / \mathrm{KOH} / \Delta$ produces R-NC.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
Statement I: The dipole moment of alkyl cyanides ($R-CN$) is indeed greater than alkyl isocyanides ($R-NC$) because the $C \equiv N$ bond is more polar than the $N^+ \equiv C^-$ bond where formal charges oppose the electronegativity difference. However, the acidic hydrolysis of $R-NC$ produces a primary amine ($R-NH_2$) and formic acid ($HCOOH$), not a carboxylic acid ($R-COOH$). Thus, Statement I is false.
Statement II: $R-CN$ on acidic hydrolysis gives $R-COOH$. Treatment with $SOCl_2$ gives $R-COCl$. Addition of $NH_3$ gives $R-CONH_2$ (compound x). Treatment of $R-CONH_2$ with $NaOCl/NaOH$ (Hofmann Bromamide Degradation equivalent) gives $R-NH_2$. $R-NH_2$ on treatment with $CHCl_3/KOH/\Delta$ (Carbylamine reaction) produces $R-NC$. Thus, Statement II is true.
Therefore, Statement I is false but Statement II is true.

q: A student has planned to prepare acetanilide from aniline using acetic anhydride. The student has started from 9.3 g of aniline. However, the student has managed to obtain 11 g of dry acetanilide. The % yield of this reaction is :-
question: A student has planned to prepare acetanilide from aniline using acetic anhydride. The student has started from 9.3 g of aniline. However, the student has managed to obtain 11 g of dry acetanilide. The % yield of this reaction is :-
solution:
Molar mass of aniline ($C_6H_5NH_2$) = 93 g/mol.
Moles of aniline = 9.3/93 = 0.1 mol.
From the reaction, theoretical yield of acetanilide = 0.1 mol.
Molar mass of acetanilide ($C_6H_5NHCOCH_3$) = 135 g/mol.
Theoretical mass = 0.1 × 135 = 13.5 g.
Percentage yield = (11/13.5) × 100 = 81.5%

q: The number of possible tripeptides formed involving alanine (ala), glycine (gly) and valine (val), where no amino acid has been used more than once is:
question: The number of possible tripeptides formed involving alanine (ala), glycine (gly) and valine (val), where no amino acid has been used more than once is:
solution: For tripeptides with 3 different amino acids (ala, gly, val) where no amino acid is used more than once, each tripeptide must contain all three amino acids exactly once. The number of distinct tripeptides is the number of permutations of 3 amino acids = 3! = 6. These are: ala-gly-val, ala-val-gly, gly-ala-val, gly-val-ala, val-ala-gly, val-gly-ala.

Consider the following gaseous equilibrium in a closed container of volume ' V ' at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$.
$\mathrm{P}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Q}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{PQ}(\mathrm{g})$
2 moles each of $\mathrm{P}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{Q}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{PQ}(\mathrm{g})$ are present at equilibrium. Now one mole each of ' $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ ' and ' $\mathrm{Q}_{2}$ ' are added to the equilibrium keeping the temperature at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$. The number of moles of $P_{2}, Q_{2}$ and $P Q$ at the new equilibrium, respectively, are
question:
Consider the following gaseous equilibrium in a closed container of volume ' V ' at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$.
$\mathrm{P}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Q}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{PQ}(\mathrm{g})$
2 moles each of $\mathrm{P}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{Q}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{PQ}(\mathrm{g})$ are present at equilibrium. Now one mole each of ' $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ ' and ' $\mathrm{Q}_{2}$ ' are added to the equilibrium keeping the temperature at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$. The number of moles of $P_{2}, Q_{2}$ and $P Q$ at the new equilibrium, respectively, are
solution:
Initial equilibrium: 2 moles each of $P_2$, $Q_2$, $PQ$ with $K = \frac{[PQ]^2}{[P_2][Q_2]} = \frac{4}{4} = 1$.
After adding 1 mole each of $P_2$ and $Q_2$: initial composition is $(3, 3, 2)$. Reaction quotient $Q = \frac{4}{9} < K$, so reaction shifts right.
Let x moles of reactants combine:
$\frac{(2+2x)^2}{(3-x)^2} = 1$
$2+2x = 3-x$
$3x = 1$ → $x = \frac{1}{3}$
Final moles: $P_2 = 3 - \frac{1}{3} = 2.67$, $Q_2 = 2.67$, $PQ = 2 + \frac{2}{3} = 2.67$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The half-life of ${ }^{65} \mathrm{Zn}$ is 245 days. After $x$ days, $75 \%$ of original activity remained. The value of $x$ in days is $\_\_\_\_$. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $\log 3=0.4771$ and $\log 2=0.3010$)
question:
The half-life of ${ }^{65} \mathrm{Zn}$ is 245 days. After $x$ days, $75 \%$ of original activity remained. The value of $x$ in days is $\_\_\_\_$. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $\log 3=0.4771$ and $\log 2=0.3010$)
solution:
Using radioactive decay: $A = A_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t/t_{1/2}}$.
With 75% activity remaining: $0.75 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{x/245}$.
Taking log base 10: $\log(0.75) = \frac{x}{245}\log(0.5)$.
Calculate: $\log(0.75) = \log 3 - \log 4 = 0.4771 - 2(0.3010) = -0.1249$ and $\log(0.5) = -0.3010$.
Thus $-0.1249 = \frac{x}{245} \times (-0.3010)$, giving $x = \frac{0.1249 \times 245}{0.3010} = 101.66 \approx 102$ days (nearest integer).

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: The correct order of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and F in terms of second ionisation potential is
question: The correct order of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and F in terms of second ionisation potential is
solution:
Second ionization potential (I₂) is the energy to remove the second electron.
After removing the first electron: C⁺ has 2p¹, N⁺ has 2p², O⁺ has 2p³ (half-filled, stable), F⁺ has 2p⁴.
C has lowest I₂ as 2p electrons are least tightly bound with lowest nuclear charge.
N has slightly higher I₂ than C due to increased nuclear charge.
F has highest I₂ due to highest effective nuclear charge.
O⁺ with half-filled 2p is relatively stable, making I₂ of O intermediate.
Order: $C < N < F < O$

q:
A chromium complex with a formula $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has a spin only magnetic moment value of 3.87 BM and its solution conductivity corresponds to $1: 2$ electrolyte. 2.75 g of the complex solution was initially passed through a cation exchanger. The solution obtained after the process was reacted with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. The amount of AgCl formed in the above process is $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
[Given: Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Cr}: 52 ; \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5, \mathrm{Ag}: 108, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{H}: 1$]
question:
A chromium complex with a formula $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has a spin only magnetic moment value of 3.87 BM and its solution conductivity corresponds to $1: 2$ electrolyte. 2.75 g of the complex solution was initially passed through a cation exchanger. The solution obtained after the process was reacted with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. The amount of AgCl formed in the above process is $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
[Given: Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Cr}: 52 ; \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5, \mathrm{Ag}: 108, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{H}: 1$]
solution:
The complex $\text{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ with magnetic moment 3.87 BM indicates $3.87 = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ giving n = 3 unpaired electrons, confirming Cr³⁺.
A 1:2 electrolyte means structure is $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O})_5\text{Cl}]^{2+}\text{Cl}_2^-$.
Molar mass = 52 + 5(18) + 35.5 + 6(18) = 285.5 g/mol.
Moles of complex = 2.75/285.5 = 0.00963 mol.
After cation exchange, 2 Cl⁻ remain per formula unit (from the 1:2 electrolyte), giving 0.00963 × 2 = 0.01926 mol of free Cl⁻.
Reacting with excess AgNO₃: mass of AgCl = 0.01926 × 143.5 = 2.76 g ≈ 3 g (nearest integer).

q: " X " is an oxoanion of the lightest element of group 7 (in the periodic table). The metal is in +6 oxidation state in " X ". The color of the potassium salt of X is
question: " X " is an oxoanion of the lightest element of group 7 (in the periodic table). The metal is in +6 oxidation state in " X ". The color of the potassium salt of X is
solution:
The lightest element of group 7 (considering transition metals) is manganese (Mn).
An oxoanion with Mn in +6 oxidation state is manganate, MnO₄²⁻.
In manganate, Mn exhibits +6 oxidation state (where oxygen is -2, so Mn + 4(-2) = -2, giving Mn = +6).
The potassium salt K₂MnO₄ (potassium manganate) is green in color.
This is distinct from permanganate KMnO₄ (where Mn is +7) which is purple.
The characteristic green color of manganate solutions and salts makes this the answer.

q:
Molar conductivity of a weak acid HQ of concentration 0.18 M was found to be $1 / 30$ of the molar conductivity of another weak acid HZ with concentration of 0.02 M. If $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Q}^{-}$happened to be equal with $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Z}^{-}$, then the difference of the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of the two weak acids $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ (Nearest integer).
[Given: degree of dissociation $(\alpha) \ll 1$ for both weak acids, $\lambda^{\circ}$ : limiting molar conductivity of ions]
question:
Molar conductivity of a weak acid HQ of concentration 0.18 M was found to be $1 / 30$ of the molar conductivity of another weak acid HZ with concentration of 0.02 M. If $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Q}^{-}$happened to be equal with $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Z}^{-}$, then the difference of the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of the two weak acids $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ (Nearest integer).
[Given: degree of dissociation $(\alpha) \ll 1$ for both weak acids, $\lambda^{\circ}$ : limiting molar conductivity of ions]
solution:
For weak acids with $\lambda^{\infty}Q^- = \lambda^{\infty}Z^-$, the molar conductivity is $\lambda_m = \alpha \lambda^{\infty}$.
From $\lambda_m^Q = \frac{1}{30}\lambda_m^Z$, we get $\alpha_Q = \frac{\alpha_Z}{30}$.
Using $K_a = \alpha^2 C$ for weak acids: $K_a(HQ) = \alpha_Q^2 \times 0.18 = \frac{\alpha_Z^2 \times 0.18}{900}$ and $K_a(HZ) = \alpha_Z^2 \times 0.02$.
Therefore $\frac{K_a(HZ)}{K_a(HQ)} = \frac{0.02 \times 900}{0.18} = 100$.
Thus $pK_a(HQ) - pK_a(HZ) = \log(100) = 2$.

question:
Find out the statements which are not true.
A. Resonating structures with more number of covalent bonds and lesser charge separation are more stable.
B. In electromeric effect, an unsaturated system shows +E effect with nucleophile and -E effect with electrophile.
C. Inductive effect is responsible for high melting point, boiling point and dipole moment of polar compounds.
D. The greater the number of alkyl groups attached to the doubly bonded carbon atoms, higher is the heat of hydrogenation.
E. Stability of carbanion increases with the increase in $\mathrm{s}-$ character of the carbon carrying the negative charge.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Evaluating each statement:
(A) TRUE - More covalent bonds with lesser charge separation increases resonance stabilization.
(B) FALSE - In electromeric effect, unsaturated systems show +E effect toward nucleophiles and -E effect toward electrophiles, opposite to stated.
(C) TRUE - Inductive effect of polar groups affects melting points, boiling points, and dipole moments.
(D) FALSE - Greater alkyl substitution on doubly bonded carbons decreases heat of hydrogenation (more substituted alkenes are more stable).
(E) TRUE - Higher s-character of orbital holding negative charge increases carbanion stability.

Statements NOT true: B and D. Therefore answer is B & D only.

q:
0.25 g of an organic compound " A " containing carbon, hydrogen and oxygen was analysed using the combustion method. There was an increase in mass of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ tube and potash tube at the end of the experiment. The amount was found to be 0.15 g and 0.1837 g, respectively. The percentage of oxygen in compound A is %. (Nearest integer)
(Given: molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
question:
0.25 g of an organic compound " A " containing carbon, hydrogen and oxygen was analysed using the combustion method. There was an increase in mass of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ tube and potash tube at the end of the experiment. The amount was found to be 0.15 g and 0.1837 g, respectively. The percentage of oxygen in compound A is %. (Nearest integer)
(Given: molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
solution:
In combustion analysis: CaCl₂ tube absorbs H₂O and potash tube absorbs CO₂.
Mass increase of CaCl₂ = 0.15 g gives moles of H₂O = 0.15/18 = 0.00833 mol, so moles of H = 0.01667 mol and mass of H = 0.01667 g.
Mass increase of potash = 0.1837 g gives moles of CO₂ = 0.1837/44 = 0.00417 mol, so moles of C = 0.00417 mol and mass of C = 0.05 g.
Mass of O = 0.25 - 0.05 - 0.01667 = 0.18333 g.
Percentage of O = (0.18333/0.25) × 100 = 73.33% ≈ 73% (nearest integer).

q: Grignard reagent $\mathrm{RMgBr}(\mathrm{P})$ reacts with water and forms a gas (Q). One gram of Q occupies $1.4 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ at STP. (P) on reaction with dry ice in dry ether followed by $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$forms a compound (Z). 0.1 mole of (Z) will weigh $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
question: Grignard reagent $\mathrm{RMgBr}(\mathrm{P})$ reacts with water and forms a gas (Q). One gram of Q occupies $1.4 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ at STP. (P) on reaction with dry ice in dry ether followed by $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$forms a compound (Z). 0.1 mole of (Z) will weigh $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
solution:
Grignard reagent RMgBr reacts with water to produce gas Q.
One gram of Q occupies 1.4 L at STP.
Moles of Q = 1.4/22.4 = 0.0625 mol, so molar mass = 1/0.0625 = 16 g/mol, identifying Q as CH₄.
Thus P = CH₃MgBr.
When P reacts with dry ice: $\text{CH}_3\text{MgBr} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOMgBr}$.
Upon treatment with H₃O⁺, this gives Z = CH₃COOH (acetic acid).
Molar mass of Z = 60 g/mol.
Mass of 0.1 mol = 0.1 × 60 = 6 g.

What is the mole fraction of $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ in water at 298 K ? ($\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}$ for $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is $6.5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$)
question: At 298 K, the mole percentage of $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ in air is $80 \%$. Water is in equilibrium with air at a pressure of 10 atm. What is the mole fraction of $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ in water at 298 K ? ($\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}$ for $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is $6.5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$)
solution:
Using Henry's Law: Mole fraction = $\frac{P}{K_H}$
N₂ mole percentage in air = 80%, total pressure = 10 atm
Partial pressure of N₂ = 0.80 × 10 = 8 atm = 8 × 760 = 6080 mmHg
$K_H$ for N₂ = 6.5 × 10⁷ mmHg
Mole fraction of N₂ = $\frac{6080}{6.5 \times 10^7} = \frac{6.08 \times 10^3}{6.5 \times 10^7} = 0.935 \times 10^{-4} = 9.35 \times 10^{-5}$

q: Two liquids A and B form an ideal solution at temperature T K. At T K, the vapour pressures of pure A and B are 55 and $15 \mathrm{kN} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ respectively. What is the mole fraction of $A$ in solution of $A$ and $B$ in equilibrium with a vapour in which the mole fraction of A is 0.8 ?
question: Two liquids A and B form an ideal solution at temperature T K. At T K, the vapour pressures of pure A and B are 55 and $15 \mathrm{kN} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ respectively. What is the mole fraction of $A$ in solution of $A$ and $B$ in equilibrium with a vapour in which the mole fraction of A is 0.8 ?
solution:
For an ideal solution with Raoult's law, the partial pressure of A in vapour is $P_A = P_A^0 y_A$ where $y_A = 0.8$ is the mole fraction in vapour phase. Thus $P_A = 55 \times 0.8 = 44$ kNm$^{-2}$.
Using the equilibrium condition $y_A = \frac{P_A^0 \chi_A}{P_{total}}$ where $\chi_A$ is mole fraction in liquid and $P_{total} = P_A^0 \chi_A + P_B^0(1-\chi_A)$:
$0.8 = \frac{55\chi_A}{55\chi_A + 15(1-\chi_A)}$
$0.8(55\chi_A + 15 - 15\chi_A) = 55\chi_A$
$44\chi_A + 12 - 12\chi_A = 55\chi_A$
$12 = 23\chi_A$
$\chi_A = 0.5217$

question: The wavelength of spectral line obtained in the spectrum of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ ion, when the transition takes place between two levels whose sum is 4 and difference is 2, is
solution:
For Li²⁺ (Z=3): From $n_2 + n_1 = 4$ and $n_2 - n_1 = 2$, we get $n_2 = 3$, $n_1 = 1$
Using Bohr wavelength formula: $\frac{1}{\lambda} = RZ^2\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$
$\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \times 9 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{9}\right) = 9.873 \times 10^7 \times \frac{8}{9}$
$\frac{1}{\lambda} = 8.776 \times 10^7$ m⁻¹
$\lambda = 1.14 \times 10^{-6}$ cm

q: The heat of atomisation of methane and ethane are ' x ' $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ and ' y ' $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The longest wavelength $(\lambda)$ of light capable of breaking the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond can be expressed in SI unit as:
question: The heat of atomisation of methane and ethane are ' x ' $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ and ' y ' $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The longest wavelength $(\lambda)$ of light capable of breaking the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond can be expressed in SI unit as:
solution:
Heat of atomization: CH₄ has x kJ/mol (4 C-H bonds), C₂H₆ has y kJ/mol (1 C-C + 6 C-H bonds)
Let C-H bond energy = a, C-C bond energy = b
Then: x = 4a and y = b + 6a
Solving: a = x/4, so b = y - 6x/4 = (4y - 6x)/4 kJ/mol
Converting to J/mol: b = (4y - 6x) × 250 J/mol
Using $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ and $\lambda = \frac{hc}{E}$:
$\lambda = \frac{N_A hc}{250(4y - 6x)}$ (in SI units)

question:
Consider the following three statements for the function $f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x)=\left|\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right|-|x-1|$ :
(I) $f$ is differentiable at all $x>0$.
(II) $f$ is increasing in $(0,1)$.
(III) $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Then.
solution:
For $0 < x < 1$: $f(x) = -\ln x + x - 1$, $f'(x) = -\frac{1}{x} + 1 = \frac{x-1}{x} < 0$ (decreasing).
For $x > 1$: $f(x) = \ln x - x + 1$, $f'(x) = \frac{1}{x} - 1 = \frac{1-x}{x} < 0$ (decreasing).
At $x = 1$: LHD $= \frac{1-1}{1} = 0$, RHD $= \frac{1-1}{1} = 0$. So $f$ is differentiable at $x = 1$.
(I) TRUE: $f$ is differentiable for all $x > 0$.
(II) FALSE: $f$ is decreasing in $(0, 1)$.
(III) TRUE: $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Only (I) and (III) are TRUE.

question: Let $f(\alpha)$ denote the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by $x=0, x=1, y^{2}=x$ and $y=|\alpha x-5|-|1-\alpha x|+\alpha x^{2}$. Then $(f(0)+f(1))$ is equal to
solution:
For $\alpha = 0$: curve is $y = 5 - 1 = 4$ (horizontal line), area $= \int_0^1 (4 - \sqrt{x}) dx = 4 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{10}{3}$
For $\alpha = 1$ and $0 \leq x \leq 1$: $|x-5| = 5-x$, $|1-x| = 1-x$, so $y = 4 + x^2$
Area $= \int_0^1 (4 + x^2 - \sqrt{x}) dx = 4 + \frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{11}{3}$
$f(0) + f(1) = \frac{10}{3} + \frac{11}{3} = 7$

question: Let $z=(1+i)(1+2 i)(1+3 i) \ldots(1+n i)$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$. If $|z|^{2}=44200$, then $n$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
$z = (1 + i)(1 + 2i)(1 + 3i)\cdots(1 + ni)$ where $i = \sqrt{-1}$
$|z|^2 = \prod_{k=1}^{n} |1 + ki|^2 = \prod_{k=1}^{n} (1 + k^2)$
Computing: $(1+1)(1+4)(1+9)(1+16)(1+25) = 2 \times 5 \times 10 \times 17 \times 26$
$= 10 \times 10 \times 17 \times 26 = 100 \times 442 = 44200$
Therefore $n = 5$

question: Let $[t]$ denote the greatest integer less than or equal to $t$. If the function $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}b^{2} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}(\cos x+\sin x) \cos x\right]\right), & x<0 \\ \frac{\sin x-\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x}{x^{3}} &, x>0 \\ a &, x=0\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=0$, then $a^{2}+b^{2}$ is equal to
solution:
For $x > 0$: $\sin x - \frac{1}{2}\sin 2x = \sin x(1 - \cos x)$.
$\lim_{x \to 0^+} \frac{\sin x(1-\cos x)}{x^3} = \lim_{x \to 0^+} \frac{\sin x}{x} \cdot \frac{1-\cos x}{x^2} = 1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}$.
So $a = \frac{1}{2}$.
For $x < 0$ near $0$: $\frac{\pi}{2}(\cos x + \sin x)\cos x \to \frac{\pi}{2}$ from below, so $\left[\frac{\pi}{2}(\cos x + \sin x)\cos x\right] = 1$.
$f(x) = b^2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 1\right) = b^2$.
For continuity: $b^2 = a = \frac{1}{2}$.
$a^2 + b^2 = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{4}$.

q: If $f(x)$ satisfies the relation $f(x)=e^{x}+\int_{0}^{1}\left(y+x e^{x}\right) f(y) d y$, then $e+f(0)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: If $f(x)$ satisfies the relation $f(x)=e^{x}+\int_{0}^{1}\left(y+x e^{x}\right) f(y) d y$, then $e+f(0)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$f(x) = e^x + \displaystyle\int_0^1 (y + xe^x)f(y)\, dy$

Splitting the integral:
$f(x) = e^x + \displaystyle\int_0^1 yf(y)\, dy + xe^x \int_0^1 f(y)\, dy$

Let $A = \displaystyle\int_0^1 yf(y)\, dy$ and $B = \displaystyle\int_0^1 f(y)\, dy$

$\Rightarrow f(x) = (1 + Bx)e^x + A$

At $x = 0$: $f(0) = 1 + A$

Now compute $B$ using the expression for $f(y)$:
$B = \displaystyle\int_0^1 \left[(1 + By)e^y + A\right] dy$
$B = \displaystyle\int_0^1 e^y\, dy + B\int_0^1 ye^y\, dy + \int_0^1 A\, dy$

Evaluating each integral:
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 e^y\, dy = e - 1$
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 ye^y\, dy = [ye^y]_0^1 - \int_0^1 e^y\, dy = e - (e - 1) = 1$
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 A\, dy = A$

$\Rightarrow B = (e - 1) + B(1) + A$
$\Rightarrow 0 = e - 1 + A$
$\Rightarrow A = 1 - e$

$f(0) = 1 + A = 1 + (1 - e) = 2 - e$

$e + f(0) = e + (2 - e) = 2$

Answer: $2$

q: Let $y=y(x)$ be a differentiable function in the interval $(0, \infty)$ such that $y(1)=2$, and $\lim _{t \rightarrow x}\left(\frac{t^{2} y(x)-x^{2} y(t)}{x-t}\right)=3$ for each $x>0$. Then $2 y(2)$ is equal to
question: Let $y=y(x)$ be a differentiable function in the interval $(0, \infty)$ such that $y(1)=2$, and $\lim _{t \rightarrow x}\left(\frac{t^{2} y(x)-x^{2} y(t)}{x-t}\right)=3$ for each $x>0$. Then $2 y(2)$ is equal to
solution:
Given the limit $\lim_{t \to x} \frac{t^2 y(x) - x^2 y(t)}{x - t} = 3$.
The limit is in $\frac{0}{0}$ form. Applying L'Hopital's Rule with respect to $t$:
$\lim_{t \to x} \frac{\frac{d}{dt}(t^2 y(x) - x^2 y(t))}{\frac{d}{dt}(x - t)} = 3$
$\lim_{t \to x} \frac{2t y(x) - x^2 y'(t)}{-1} = 3$
Substituting $t = x$:
$\frac{2x y(x) - x^2 y'(x)}{-1} = 3$
$x^2 y'(x) - 2x y(x) = 3$
Dividing by $x^4$ to make it a linear differential equation or recognizing the quotient rule form:
$\frac{x^2 y'(x) - 2x y(x)}{x^4} = \frac{3}{x^4}$
$\frac{d}{dx} \left( \frac{y(x)}{x^2} \right) = \frac{3}{x^4}$
Integrating both sides with respect to $x$:
$\frac{y(x)}{x^2} = \int 3x^{-4} dx = \frac{3x^{-3}}{-3} + C = -\frac{1}{x^3} + C$
Given $y(1) = 2$:
$\frac{2}{1^2} = -\frac{1}{1^3} + C \implies 2 = -1 + C \implies C = 3$
So, $\frac{y(x)}{x^2} = -\frac{1}{x^3} + 3$
$y(x) = -\frac{1}{x} + 3x^2$
We need to find $2y(2)$:
$y(2) = -\frac{1}{2} + 3(2^2) = -\frac{1}{2} + 12 = \frac{23}{2}$
$2y(2) = 2 \times \frac{23}{2} = 23$

q: Let $(h, k)$ lie on the circle $\mathrm{C}: x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ and the point $(2 h+1,3 k+2)$ lie on an ellipse with eccentricity $e$. Then the value of $\frac{5}{e^{2}}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Let $(h, k)$ lie on the circle $\mathrm{C}: x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ and the point $(2 h+1,3 k+2)$ lie on an ellipse with eccentricity $e$. Then the value of $\frac{5}{e^{2}}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Since $(h, k)$ lies on circle $x^2 + y^2 = 4$, we have $h = 2\cos\theta$ and $k = 2\sin\theta$.
The transformed point is $(2h+1, 3k+2) = (4\cos\theta + 1, 6\sin\theta + 2)$.
Rearranging: $\frac{(x-1)^2}{16} + \frac{(y-2)^2}{36} = 1$
This is an ellipse with $a^2 = 36$, $b^2 = 16$, so $e^2 = 1 - \frac{b^2}{a^2} = 1 - \frac{16}{36} = \frac{5}{9}$
Therefore $\frac{5}{e^2} = \frac{5}{5/9} = 9$

question: Let $f$ be a function such that $3 f(x)+2 f\left(\frac{m}{19 x}\right)=5 x, x \neq 0$, where $m=\sum_{i=1}^{9}(i)^{2}$. Then $f(5)-f(2)$ is equal to
solution:
$m = \sum_{i=1}^{9} i^2 = \frac{9 \cdot 10 \cdot 19}{6} = 285$, so $\frac{m}{19} = 15$.
The equation becomes $3f(x) + 2f\left(\frac{15}{x}\right) = 5x$ ...(i)
Replace $x$ by $\frac{15}{x}$: $3f\left(\frac{15}{x}\right) + 2f(x) = \frac{75}{x}$ ...(ii)
From $3 \times (i) - 2 \times (ii)$: $5f(x) = 15x - \frac{150}{x}$, so $f(x) = 3x - \frac{30}{x}$.
$f(5) = 15 - 6 = 9$, $f(2) = 6 - 15 = -9$.
$f(5) - f(2) = 9 - (-9) = 18$.

question: If the domain of the function $f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}-2 x-2}\right)$, is $(-\infty, \alpha] \cup[\beta, \gamma] \cup[\delta, \infty)$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta$ is equal to
solution:
For $\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{x^2-2x-2}\right)$ to be defined, we need $\left|\frac{1}{x^2-2x-2}\right| \leq 1$, i.e., $|x^2-2x-2| \geq 1$.
Case 1:
$x^2-2x-2 \geq 1 \Rightarrow x^2-2x-3 \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow (x-3)(x+1) \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow x \leq -1$ or $x \geq 3$.
Case 2:
$x^2-2x-2 \leq -1 \Rightarrow x^2-2x-1 \leq 0$
$\Rightarrow (x-1)^2 \leq 2$
$\Rightarrow 1-\sqrt{2} \leq x \leq 1+\sqrt{2}$.
Domain $= (-\infty, -1] \cup [1-\sqrt{2}, 1+\sqrt{2}] \cup [3, \infty)$.
So $\alpha = -1,\ \beta = 1-\sqrt{2},\ \gamma = 1+\sqrt{2},\ \delta = 3$.
$\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = -1 + 1 - \sqrt{2} + 1 + \sqrt{2} + 3 = 4$.

question: Let $f(x)=\int \frac{7 x^{10}+9 x^{8}}{\left(1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}\right)^{2}} d x, x>0, \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=0$ and $f(1)=\frac{1}{4}$. If $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 0 & 1 \\ \frac{1}{4} & f^{\prime}(1) & 1 \\ \alpha^{2} & 4 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})$ be such that $|\mathrm{B}|=81$, then $\alpha^{2}$ is equal to
solution:
Divide numerator and denominator by $x^{18}$, let $t = x^{-9} + x^{-7} + 2$, then $dt = -(9x^{-10}+7x^{-8})dx$.
$f(x) = \frac{1}{t} + C = \frac{x^9}{1+x^2+2x^9} + C$.
$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow C = 0$. $f(1) = \frac{1}{4}$ (verified).
$f'(x) = \frac{9x^8(1+x^2+2x^9) - x^9(2x+18x^8)}{(1+x^2+2x^9)^2}$.
$f'(1) = \frac{36-20}{16} = 1$.
$|A| = \begin{vmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1/4 & 1 & 1 \\ \alpha^2 & 4 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 1 - \alpha^2$.
$|B| = |\text{adj}(\text{adj } A)| = |A|^{(3-1)^2} = (1-\alpha^2)^4 = 81 = 3^4$.
$|1-\alpha^2| = 3 \Rightarrow \alpha^2 = 4$.

solution:
Reflection of point $(h, k)$ in line $x - y - 1 = 0$:
$\frac{x-h}{1} = \frac{y-k}{-1} = \frac{-2(h-k-1)}{2} = k - h + 1$.
Image: $(k+1,\ h-1)$.
Let $(X, Y) = (k+1,\ h-1)$, so $h = Y+1,\ k = X-1$.
From $h^2 = 4k$: $(Y+1)^2 = 4(X-1)$.
Comparing with $(y+a)^2 = b(x-c)$: $a = 1,\ b = 4,\ c = 1$.
$a + b + c = 1 + 4 + 1 = 6$.

q: The largest value of $n$, for which $40^{n}$ divides $60!$, is
question: The largest value of $n$, for which $40^{n}$ divides $60!$, is
solution:
$40^n = 2^{3n} \times 5^n$. We need the highest power of $40$ dividing $60!$.
Power of $5$ in $60! = \lfloor 60/5 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/25 \rfloor = 12 + 2 = 14$.
Power of $2$ in $60! = \lfloor 60/2 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/4 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/8 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/16 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/32 \rfloor = 30 + 15 + 7 + 3 + 1 = 56$.
We need $n \leq 14$ and $3n \leq 56$ (i.e., $n \leq 18$).
The binding constraint is $n \leq 14$, so the largest value is $n = 14$.

q: The letters of the word "UDAYPUR" are written in all possible ways with or without meaning and these words are arranged as in a dictionary. The rank of the word "UDAYPUR" is
question: The letters of the word "UDAYPUR" are written in all possible ways with or without meaning and these words are arranged as in a dictionary. The rank of the word "UDAYPUR" is
solution:
Letters of UDAYPUR: A, D, P, R, U, U, Y (U repeated twice).
Words starting with a letter before U: letters A, D, P, R each give $\frac{6!}{2!} = 360$ arrangements $\Rightarrow 4 \times 360 = 1440$.
Fix U at position 1. Remaining: A, D, P, R, U, Y (all distinct).
Words starting with UA: $5! = 120$.
Fix UD at positions 1-2. Remaining: A, P, R, U, Y. No letter before A, so fix UDA.
Remaining: P, R, U, Y. Letters before Y: P, R, U give $3 \times 3! = 18$.
Fix UDAY. Remaining: P, R, U. No letter before P, fix UDAYP.
Remaining: R, U. Letter before U: R gives $1! = 1$.
Fix UDAYPU, last letter R: the word itself.
Rank $= 1440 + 120 + 18 + 1 + 1 = 1580$.

question: Let S be a set of 5 elements and $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S})$ denote the power set of S. Let E be an event of choosing an ordered pair ($\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$) from the set $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S}) \times \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S})$ such that $\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B}=\emptyset$. If the probability of the event $E$ is $\frac{3^{p}}{2^{q}}$, where $p, q \in N$, then $p+q$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
$S$ has 5 elements, so $P(S)$ has $2^5 = 32$ subsets.
Total ordered pairs from $P(S) \times P(S)$ is $32^2 = 1024$
For pairs $(A, B)$ with $A \cap B = \emptyset$: each of the 5 elements has 3 choices - in $A$ only, in $B$ only, or in neither
Number of favorable outcomes = $3^5 = 243$
Probability $= \frac{243}{1024} = \frac{3^5}{2^{10}}$
With $p = 5$ and $q = 10$, we get $p + q = 15$

question: The smallest positive integral value of $a$, for which all the roots of $x^{4}-a x^{2}+9=0$ are real and distinct, is equal to
solution:
Let $t = x^2$, so the equation becomes $t^2 - at + 9 = 0$.
For all four roots of the original equation to be real and distinct, both roots of this quadratic must be positive and distinct.
Discriminant $> 0$: $a^2 - 36 > 0 \Rightarrow |a| > 6$. Since $a > 0$, we need $a > 6$.
Product of roots $= 9 > 0$ and sum of roots $= a > 0$, so both roots are positive.
The smallest positive integer satisfying $a > 6$ is $a = 7$.

q: Let $a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, a_{4}$ be an A.P. of four terms such that each term of the A.P. and its common difference $l$ are integers. If $a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}=48$ and $a_{1} a_{2} a_{3} a_{4}+l^{4}=361$, then the largest term of the A.P. is equal to
question: Let $a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, a_{4}$ be an A.P. of four terms such that each term of the A.P. and its common difference $l$ are integers. If $a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}=48$ and $a_{1} a_{2} a_{3} a_{4}+l^{4}=361$, then the largest term of the A.P. is equal to
solution:
Let the four terms be $12 - 3k,\ 12 - k,\ 12 + k,\ 12 + 3k$ with common difference $l = 2k$.
Sum $= 48$ is satisfied. Product condition:
$(144 - 9k^2)(144 - k^2) + 16k^4 = 361$
$25k^4 - 1440k^2 + 20375 = 0$
Let $u = k^2$: $25u^2 - 1440u + 20375 = 0$
$u = \frac{1440 \pm \sqrt{2073600 - 2037500}}{50} = \frac{1440 \pm 190}{50}$
$u = 25$ (integer) or $u = 32.6$ (rejected).
$k = 5$, $l = 10$. Terms: $-3, 7, 17, 27$.
Largest term $= 27$.

subject: Mathematics
q: $\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{4}{7}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{7}+\frac{4^{2}}{7^{2}}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3^{3}}+\frac{1}{3^{2}} \times \frac{4}{7}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4^{2}}{7^{2}}+\frac{4^{3}}{7^{3}}\right)+\ldots$ upto infinite terms, is equal to
question: $\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{4}{7}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{7}+\frac{4^{2}}{7^{2}}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3^{3}}+\frac{1}{3^{2}} \times \frac{4}{7}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4^{2}}{7^{2}}+\frac{4^{3}}{7^{3}}\right)+\ldots$ upto infinite terms, is equal to
solution:
The $n$-th bracket ($n \geq 1$) is $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{n-k}\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)^k = \frac{(4/7)^{n+1} - (1/3)^{n+1}}{4/7 - 1/3} = \frac{21}{5}\left[\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)^{n+1} - \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{n+1}\right]$.
Total sum $= \frac{21}{5}\left[\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)^n - \sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n\right] = \frac{21}{5}\left[\frac{16/49}{3/7} - \frac{1/9}{2/3}\right]$
$= \frac{21}{5}\left[\frac{16}{21} - \frac{1}{6}\right] = \frac{21}{5} \cdot \frac{25}{42} = \frac{5}{2}$.

q: Let $\mathrm{X}=\{x \in \mathrm{~N}: 1 \leq x \leq 19\}$ and for some $a, b \in \mathbb{R}, \mathrm{Y}=\{a x+b: x \in \mathrm{X}\}$. If the mean and variance of the elements of Y are 30 and 750, respectively, then the sum of all possible values of $b$ is
question: Let $\mathrm{X}=\{x \in \mathrm{~N}: 1 \leq x \leq 19\}$ and for some $a, b \in \mathbb{R}, \mathrm{Y}=\{a x+b: x \in \mathrm{X}\}$. If the mean and variance of the elements of Y are 30 and 750, respectively, then the sum of all possible values of $b$ is
solution:
Mean of $X = \frac{1+19}{2} = 10$, Variance of $X = \frac{19^2-1}{12} = 30$.
For $Y = aX + b$: Mean of $Y = 10a + b = 30$ and Variance of $Y = a^2 \cdot 30 = 750$.
$a^2 = 25 \Rightarrow a = 5$ or $a = -5$.
If $a = 5$: $b = 30 - 50 = -20$.
If $a = -5$: $b = 30 + 50 = 80$.
Sum of all possible values of $b = -20 + 80 = 60$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the angles made with the positive $x$-axis by two straight lines drawn from the point $\mathrm{P}(2,3)$ and meeting the line $x+y=6$ at a distance $\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ from the point P be $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$. Then the value of $\left(\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}\right)$ is:
question: Let the angles made with the positive $x$-axis by two straight lines drawn from the point $\mathrm{P}(2,3)$ and meeting the line $x+y=6$ at a distance $\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ from the point P be $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$. Then the value of $\left(\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}\right)$ is:
solution:
Using parametric form through $P(2,3)$: point at distance $r = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ is $(2 + r\cos\theta,\ 3 + r\sin\theta)$.
Substituting in $x + y = 6$: $r(\cos\theta + \sin\theta) = 1$, so $\cos\theta + \sin\theta = \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$.
$\sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \Rightarrow \sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
$\theta + \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{3}$ or $\frac{2\pi}{3}$.
$\theta_1 = \frac{\pi}{12},\ \theta_2 = \frac{5\pi}{12}$.
$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = \frac{\pi}{12} + \frac{5\pi}{12} = \frac{\pi}{2}$.

q: The sum of all values of $\alpha$, for which the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x+1}{\alpha}=\frac{y-2}{-1}=\frac{z-4}{-\alpha}$ and $\frac{x}{\alpha}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{2 \alpha}$ is $\sqrt{2}$, is
question: The sum of all values of $\alpha$, for which the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x+1}{\alpha}=\frac{y-2}{-1}=\frac{z-4}{-\alpha}$ and $\frac{x}{\alpha}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{2 \alpha}$ is $\sqrt{2}$, is
solution:
The given lines are $L_1: \frac{x+1}{\alpha} = \frac{y-2}{-1} = \frac{z-4}{-\alpha}$ and $L_2: \frac{x}{\alpha} = \frac{y-1}{2} = \frac{z-1}{2\alpha}$.
For $L_1$, point $\vec{a}_1 = (-1, 2, 4)$ and direction $\vec{b}_1 = (\alpha, -1, -\alpha)$.
For $L_2$, point $\vec{a}_2 = (0, 1, 1)$ and direction $\vec{b}_2 = (\alpha, 2, 2\alpha)$.
The shortest distance $d$ is given by $d = \frac{|(\vec{a}_2 - \vec{a}_1) \cdot (\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2)|}{|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2|}$.
$\vec{a}_2 - \vec{a}_1 = (1, -1, -3)$.
$\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2 = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \alpha & -1 & -\alpha \\ \alpha & 2 & 2\alpha \end{vmatrix} = \hat{i}(-2\alpha + 2\alpha) - \hat{j}(2\alpha^2 + \alpha^2) + \hat{k}(2\alpha + \alpha) = (0, -3\alpha^2, 3\alpha)$.
$|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2| = \sqrt{0 + 9\alpha^4 + 9\alpha^2} = 3|\alpha|\sqrt{\alpha^2 + 1}$.
$(\vec{a}_2 - \vec{a}_1) \cdot (\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2) = (1)(0) + (-1)(-3\alpha^2) + (-3)(3\alpha) = 3\alpha^2 - 9\alpha$.
Given $d = \sqrt{2}$, so $\frac{|3\alpha^2 - 9\alpha|}{3|\alpha|\sqrt{\alpha^2 + 1}} = \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \frac{|\alpha - 3|}{\sqrt{\alpha^2 + 1}} = \sqrt{2}$.
Squaring both sides: $(\alpha - 3)^2 = 2(\alpha^2 + 1) \Rightarrow \alpha^2 - 6\alpha + 9 = 2\alpha^2 + 2$.
$\alpha^2 + 6\alpha - 7 = 0$.
The values of $\alpha$ are the roots of this quadratic equation.
Sum of values of $\alpha = -\frac{6}{1} = -6$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of elements in the set $\left\{x \in\left[0,180^{\circ}\right]: \tan \left(x+100^{\circ}\right)=\tan \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \tan x \tan \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)\right\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The number of elements in the set $\left\{x \in\left[0,180^{\circ}\right]: \tan \left(x+100^{\circ}\right)=\tan \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \tan x \tan \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)\right\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$\frac{\tan(x+100°)}{\tan x} = \tan(x+50°)\tan(x-50°)$
$\frac{\sin(x+100°)\cos x}{\cos(x+100°)\sin x} = \frac{\sin(x+50°)\sin(x-50°)}{\cos(x+50°)\cos(x-50°)}$
Applying Componendo & Dividendo:
$\frac{\sin(2x+100°)}{\sin 100°} = \frac{\cos 100°}{-\cos 2x}$
$2\sin(2x+100°)\cos 2x + \sin 200° = 0$
$\sin(4x+100°) + \sin 100° + \sin 200° = 0$
$\sin(4x+100°) = -2\sin 150° \cos 50°$
$\sin(4x+100°) = -\cos 50° = \sin(-40°)$
$\therefore 4x + 100° = n\pi + (-1)^n(-40°)$
$x = \frac{n\pi + (-1)^{n+1}(40°) - 100°}{4}$
$\therefore x = 30°, 55°, 120°, 145°$ in $(0, \pi)$
$\therefore$ Number of solutions $= 4$

q: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\vec{c}$ is a vector such that $2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97$, then $|\vec{c} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}$ is equal to
question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\vec{c}$ is a vector such that $2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97$, then $|\vec{c} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}$ is equal to
solution:
$2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c}) + 3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) = \vec{0} \Rightarrow (2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}) \times \vec{c} = \vec{0}$.
So $\vec{c} \parallel (2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}) = (7, -13, 19)$. Let $\vec{c} = t(7, -13, 19)$.
$\vec{a} - \vec{b} = (1, -4, 2)$.
$(\vec{a} - \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c} = t(7 + 52 + 38) = 97t = -97 \Rightarrow t = -1$.
$\vec{c} = (-7, 13, -19)$.
$\vec{c} \times \hat{k} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -7 & 13 & -19 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (13, 7, 0)$.
$|\vec{c} \times \hat{k}|^2 = 169 + 49 = 218$.

q: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{c}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. Let $\vec{v}$ be the vector in the plane of the vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, such that the length of its projection on the vector $\vec{c}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$. Then $|\vec{v}|$ is equal to
question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{c}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. Let $\vec{v}$ be the vector in the plane of the vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, such that the length of its projection on the vector $\vec{c}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$. Then $|\vec{v}|$ is equal to
solution:
$\vec{v} = \lambda\vec{a} + \mu\vec{b} = (2\lambda+\mu,\ -\lambda+3\mu,\ -\lambda-\mu)$.
$\vec{v} \cdot \vec{c} = 2(2\lambda+\mu) + (-\lambda+3\mu) + 3(-\lambda-\mu) = 2\mu$.
$|\vec{c}| = \sqrt{4+1+9} = \sqrt{14}$.
Projection length $= \frac{|2\mu|}{\sqrt{14}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \Rightarrow |\mu| = \frac{1}{2}$.
$|\vec{v}|^2 = 6\lambda^2 + 11\mu^2 = 6(1) + \frac{11}{4} = \frac{35}{4}$.
$|\vec{v}| = \frac{\sqrt{35}}{2}$.

q: Three parallel plate capacitors each with area $A$ and separation $d$ are filled with two dielectric ($k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$) in the following fashion. Which of the following is true? $\left(k_{1}>k_{2}\right)$
question: Three parallel plate capacitors each with area $A$ and separation $d$ are filled with two dielectric ($k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$) in the following fashion. Which of the following is true? $\left(k_{1}>k_{2}\right)$
solution:
For $C_A$

Let $\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} = C$
$\therefore C_A = \dfrac{K_1 C}{2} + \dfrac{K_1 K_2 C}{K_1 + K_2}$
$= K_1 C \left[ \dfrac{K_1 + 2K_2}{2(K_1 + K_2)} \right]$

For $C_B$

$C_B = \dfrac{K_2 C}{2} + \dfrac{K_1 K_2 C}{K_1 + K_2}$
$= K_2 C \left[ \dfrac{K_1 + 2K_2}{2(K_1 + K_2)} \right]$

For $C_C$

$\therefore C_C = \dfrac{2K_1 K_2 C}{K_1 + K_2}$
$C_A > C_C > C_B$

q: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA. The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
question: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA. The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
solution:
To convert a galvanometer to an ammeter, a shunt resistance $S$ is connected in parallel. For full-scale deflection:
Current through galvanometer: $I_g = 1$ mA
Total current: $I = 5$ mA
Current through shunt: $I_s = 5 - 1 = 4$ mA
Since voltage across both is equal: $I_g \cdot G = I_s \cdot S$
$0.001 \times 100 = 0.004 \times S$
$S = \frac{0.1}{0.004} = 25$ Ω

question: A regular hexagon is formed by six wires each of resistance $r \Omega$ and the corners are joined to the centre by wires of same resistance. If the current enters at one corner and leaves at the opposite corner, the equivalent resistance of the hexagon between the two opposite corners will be
solution: $R_{eq} = \dfrac{2R \times \dfrac{4R}{3}}{2R + \dfrac{4R}{3}} = \dfrac{8R^2}{10R} = \dfrac{4}{5}R$

q: In a meter bridge experiment to determine the value of unknown resistance, first the resistances $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the left and right gaps of the bridge and the null point is obtained at a distance $l \mathrm{~cm}$ from the left. Now when an unknown resistance $x \Omega$ is connected in parallel to $3 \Omega$ resistance, the null point is shifted by 10 cm to the right of wire. The value of unknown resistance $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: In a meter bridge experiment to determine the value of unknown resistance, first the resistances $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the left and right gaps of the bridge and the null point is obtained at a distance $l \mathrm{~cm}$ from the left. Now when an unknown resistance $x \Omega$ is connected in parallel to $3 \Omega$ resistance, the null point is shifted by 10 cm to the right of wire. The value of unknown resistance $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
Meter bridge null point condition: $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{l}{100-l}$
Initial: $R_1 = 2Ω$, $R_2 = 3Ω$, null at $l$ cm
$\frac{2}{3} = \frac{l}{100-l}$ gives $2(100-l) = 3l$, so $l = 40$ cm
When $xΩ$ connected in parallel with $3Ω$: $R_2' = \frac{3x}{3+x}Ω$
New null point at $(40+10) = 50$ cm from left:
$\frac{2}{R_2'} = \frac{50}{50} = 1$
$\frac{2(3+x)}{3x} = 1$ gives $6 + 2x = 3x$
$x = 6Ω$

q: When a light of a given wavelength falls on a metallic surface the stopping potential for photoelectrons is 3.2 V. If a second light having wavelength twice of first light is used, the stopping potential drops to 0.7 V. The wavelength of first light is $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
question: When a light of a given wavelength falls on a metallic surface the stopping potential for photoelectrons is 3.2 V. If a second light having wavelength twice of first light is used, the stopping potential drops to 0.7 V. The wavelength of first light is $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation with stopping potentials:
For first light, $h u_1 = \phi + eV_{s1}$.
For second light with $\lambda_2 = 2\lambda_1$ (so $ u_2 = u_1/2$): $h u_1/2 = \phi + eV_{s2}$.
From given values: $h u_1 - 3.2e = h u_1/2 - 0.7e$ gives $h u_1/2 = 2.5e$.
Thus $\lambda_1 = rac{hc}{5e} = rac{6.63 imes 10^{-34} imes 3 imes 10^8}{5 imes 1.6 imes 10^{-19}} \approx 2.5 imes 10^{-7}$ m.

subject: Physics
q: A point charge $q=1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is located at a distance 2 cm from one end of a thin insulating wire of length 10 cm having a charge $Q=24 \mu \mathrm{C}$, distributed uniformly along its length, as shown in figure. Force between $q$ and wire is $\_\_\_\_$ N. (Use : $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$)
question: A point charge $q=1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is located at a distance 2 cm from one end of a thin insulating wire of length 10 cm having a charge $Q=24 \mu \mathrm{C}$, distributed uniformly along its length, as shown in figure. Force between $q$ and wire is $\_\_\_\_$ N. (Use : $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$)
solution:
Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the wire at a distance $x$ from the point charge $q$.
The linear charge density of the wire is $\lambda = \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{24 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1} = 240 \times 10^{-6}$ C/m.
The charge on the small element is $dQ = \lambda dx$.
The electrostatic force between the point charge $q$ and the element $dQ$ is $dF = \frac{k q dQ}{x^2} = \frac{k q \lambda dx}{x^2}$.
The total force $F$ is obtained by integrating $dF$ from $x = a$ to $x = a + L$, where $a = 2$ cm and $L = 10$ cm.
$F = \int_{0.02}^{0.12} \frac{k q \lambda}{x^2} dx = k q \lambda \left[ -\frac{1}{x} \right]_{0.02}^{0.12}$
$F = k q \lambda \left( \frac{1}{0.02} - \frac{1}{0.12} \right) = k q \frac{Q}{L} \left( \frac{0.12 - 0.02}{0.02 \times 0.12} \right)$
$F = \frac{k q Q}{L} \frac{L}{a(a+L)} = \frac{k q Q}{a(a+L)}$
Substituting the given values: $k = 9 \times 10^9$ N.m$^2$/C$^2$, $q = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ C, $Q = 24 \times 10^{-6}$ C, $a = 0.02$ m, and $a+L = 0.12$ m.
$F = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 24 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02 \times 0.12}$
$F = \frac{9 \times 24 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0024} = \frac{216 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}$
$F = \frac{216}{2.4} = 90$ N.

q: In a vernier callipers, 50 vernier scale divisions are equal to 48 main scale divisions. If one main scale division $=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$, then the least count of the vernier callipers is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
question: In a vernier callipers, 50 vernier scale divisions are equal to 48 main scale divisions. If one main scale division $=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$, then the least count of the vernier callipers is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
solution:
Given: 50 vernier divisions = 48 main divisions and 1 main division = 0.05 mm
50 vernier divisions = 48 × 0.05 = 2.4 mm
1 vernier division = 2.4/50 = 0.048 mm
Least count = 1 main division - 1 vernier division = 0.05 - 0.048 = 0.002 mm

subject: Physics
q: A flexible chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level. The inclination of the chain with the horizontal at the two points of support is $30^{\circ}$. Considering the equilibrium of each half of the chain, the tension of the chain at the lowest point is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A flexible chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level. The inclination of the chain with the horizontal at the two points of support is $30^{\circ}$. Considering the equilibrium of each half of the chain, the tension of the chain at the lowest point is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
A chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level with inclination $30°$ at support points.
Consider equilibrium of one half of the chain (mass $m/2$):
Horizontal equilibrium: $T_0 = T_1\cos(30°) = T_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
Vertical equilibrium: $T_1\sin(30°) = \frac{m}{2}g$
From vertical equation: $T_1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{mg}{2}$, giving $T_1 = mg$
Substituting into horizontal equation: $T_0 = mg \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}mg$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
question: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
solution:
Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius $r$, are separated by distance $r$ along their common axis. Current in P is $I$ and in Q is $4I$ (both clockwise as seen from O).
Point O is at the midpoint. The magnetic fields from each loop at O:
$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from P, pointing toward Q)
$B_2 = \frac{4\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from Q, pointing toward P)
$B_{net} = B_2 - B_1 = \frac{3\mu_0 I R^2}{2(2R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{3\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}R}$
Direction: towards Q.

subject: Physics
q: A cubical block of density $\rho_{b}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ floats in a liquid of density $\rho_{\mathrm{e}}=900 \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. If the height of block is $H=8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ then height of the submerged part is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: A cubical block of density $\rho_{b}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ floats in a liquid of density $\rho_{\mathrm{e}}=900 \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. If the height of block is $H=8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ then height of the submerged part is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
For a floating cubical block in equilibrium, the buoyant force equals the weight:
$\rho_e \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h_s = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H$
where $h_s$ is the submerged height.
Simplifying: $h_s = \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_e} \cdot H = \frac{600}{900} \times 8.0 = \frac{2}{3} \times 8.0$
$= 5.33$ cm ≈ 5.3 cm

q: A soap bubble of surface tension $0.04 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is blown to a diameter of 7 cm. If $(15000-x) \mu \mathrm{J}$ of work is done in blowing it further to make its diameter 14 cm, then the value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\pi=22 / 7$)
question: A soap bubble of surface tension $0.04 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is blown to a diameter of 7 cm. If $(15000-x) \mu \mathrm{J}$ of work is done in blowing it further to make its diameter 14 cm, then the value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\pi=22 / 7$)
solution:
Soap bubble has two surfaces. Work done: $W = \Delta A \times \sigma$
Initial radius: $r_i = 3.5$ cm = 0.035 m, Final radius: $r_f = 7$ cm = 0.07 m
Change in surface area (both surfaces): $\Delta A = 8\pi(r_f^2 - r_i^2) = 8\pi(0.07^2 - 0.035^2)$
$\Delta A = 8\pi(0.0049 - 0.001225) = 8\pi \times 0.003675 = 0.0294\pi$ m²
$W = 0.0294\pi \times 0.04 = 0.001176\pi$ J = $0.001176 \times \frac{22}{7} = 0.003696$ J = 3696 μJ
Therefore: $x = 15000 - 3696 = 11304$

q: The velocity $(v)$ - Distance $(x)$ graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration $(a)$ versus distance $(x)$ variation of this system?
question: The velocity $(v)$ - Distance $(x)$ graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration $(a)$ versus distance $(x)$ variation of this system?
solution:
The v-x graph shows velocity decreasing linearly with distance. Using kinematic equation $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2ax$, we can establish the relationship between acceleration and position.
From the linear v-x relationship, differentiating using $a = v\frac{dv}{dx}$ yields acceleration that is linearly proportional to distance from the origin.
The a-x graph shows a straight line passing through the origin with positive slope, indicating that acceleration increases linearly with distance while remaining positive throughout the motion.

q: Distance between an object and three times magnified real image is 40 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: Distance between an object and three times magnified real image is 40 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
For a mirror with magnification m = -3 (real image), object and image distances satisfy |u| + |v| = 40 cm.
Since m = v/u = -3, we have v = -3u, giving u + 3u = 40, so u = 10 cm and v = -30 cm.
Using mirror formula: $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{-30} = \frac{3-1}{30} = \frac{1}{15}$
Hence f = -15 cm.

q: Five persons $\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}, \mathrm{P}_{3}, \mathrm{P}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{5}$ recorded object distance $(\mathrm{u})$ and image distance (v) using same convex lens having power +5 D as $(25,96),(30,62),(35,37),(45,35)$ and $(50,32)$ respectively. Identify correct statement
question: Five persons $\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}, \mathrm{P}_{3}, \mathrm{P}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{5}$ recorded object distance $(\mathrm{u})$ and image distance (v) using same convex lens having power +5 D as $(25,96),(30,62),(35,37),(45,35)$ and $(50,32)$ respectively. Identify correct statement
options: Readings recorded by $\mathrm{P}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ persons are incorrect
Readings recorded by $P_{4}$ and $P_{5}$ persons are incorrect
Readings recorded by all persons are correct
Readings recorded by $\mathrm{P}_{3}$ person are incorrect
solution:
For a convex lens with power $P = +5$ D, the focal length is $f = 20$ cm.
The lens formula is $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = 0.05$ cm$^{-1}$. Checking each measurement:
$P_1$: $\frac{1}{25} + \frac{1}{96} = 0.0504$ Correct;
$P_2$: $\frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{62} = 0.0494$ Correct;
$P_3$: $\frac{1}{35} + \frac{1}{37} = 0.0556$ Incorrect;
$P_4$: $\frac{1}{45} + \frac{1}{35} = 0.0508$ Correct;
$P_5$: $\frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{32} = 0.0512$ Correct.
Only $P_3$'s readings do not satisfy the lens formula.

q: A thin uniform rod $(X)$ of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at a height $\left(\frac{L}{3}\right)$ as shown in the figure. The rod is allowed to fall from a vertical position and lie horizontally on the table. The angular velocity of this rod when it hits the table top, is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\mathrm{g}=$ gravitational acceleration)
question: A thin uniform rod $(X)$ of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at a height $\left(\frac{L}{3}\right)$ as shown in the figure. The rod is allowed to fall from a vertical position and lie horizontally on the table. The angular velocity of this rod when it hits the table top, is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\mathrm{g}=$ gravitational acceleration)
solution:
The rod is pivoted at a distance $\frac{L}{3}$ from one end. The centre of mass is at a distance $\frac{L}{6}$ from the pivot.
Using energy conservation:
$mg\frac{\ell}{6} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$
Moment of inertia about the pivot (using parallel axis theorem):
$I = \frac{m\ell^2}{12} + m\left(\frac{\ell}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{m\ell^2}{12} + \frac{m\ell^2}{36} = \frac{m\ell^2}{9}$
$mg\frac{\ell}{6} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m\ell^2}{9} \cdot \omega^2$
$\omega^2 = \frac{9g}{6 \cdot \frac{\ell}{2}} = \frac{3g}{\ell}$
$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{3g}{\ell}}$

q: A uniform solid cylinder of length $L$ and radius $R$ has moment of inertia about its axis equal to $I_{1}$. A small co-centric cylinder of length $L / 2$ and radius $R / 3$ carved from this cylinder has moment of inertia about its axis equals to $I_{2}$. The ratio $I_{1} / I_{2}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A uniform solid cylinder of length $L$ and radius $R$ has moment of inertia about its axis equal to $I_{1}$. A small co-centric cylinder of length $L / 2$ and radius $R / 3$ carved from this cylinder has moment of inertia about its axis equals to $I_{2}$. The ratio $I_{1} / I_{2}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For solid cylinder: $I = \frac{1}{2}mR^2$
Large cylinder: mass $m_1 = \rho \pi R^2 L$, so $I_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^2 L \times R^2 = \frac{\rho \pi R^4 L}{2}$
Small cylinder (carved out): length L/2, radius R/3, mass $m_2 = \rho \pi(R/3)^2(L/2) = \frac{\rho \pi R^2 L}{18}$
$I_2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\rho \pi R^2 L}{18} \times (R/3)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\rho \pi R^2 L}{18} \times \frac{R^2}{9} = \frac{\rho \pi R^4 L}{324}$
$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{\rho \pi R^4 L/2}{\rho \pi R^4 L/324} = \frac{324}{2} = 162$

q: Identify the correct truth table of the given logic circuit.
question: Identify the correct truth table of the given logic circuit.
solution:
Analysing the logic circuit:

The output is $Y = A \cdot \overline{A \cdot B}$

$= A \cdot (\bar{A} + \bar{B})$

$= A\bar{A} + A\bar{B} = 0 + A\bar{B} = A\bar{B}$

Truth table:
$A=0, B=0 \Rightarrow Y=0$
$A=0, B=1 \Rightarrow Y=0$
$A=1, B=0 \Rightarrow Y=1$
$A=1, B=1 \Rightarrow Y=0$

q: 10 mole of an ideal gas is undergoing the process shown in the figure. The heat involved in the process from $P_{1}$ to $P_{2}$ is $\alpha$ Joule ($P_{1}=21.7 \mathrm{~Pa}$ and $\left.P_{2}=30 \mathrm{~Pa}, \mathrm{C}_{v}=21 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}. \mathrm{mol}, R=8.3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}. \mathrm{K}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: 10 mole of an ideal gas is undergoing the process shown in the figure. The heat involved in the process from $P_{1}$ to $P_{2}$ is $\alpha$ Joule ($P_{1}=21.7 \mathrm{~Pa}$ and $\left.P_{2}=30 \mathrm{~Pa}, \mathrm{C}_{v}=21 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}. \mathrm{mol}, R=8.3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}. \mathrm{K}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
From the given $P-V$ diagram, the process from $P_1$ to $P_2$ is an isochoric process because the volume remains constant at $V = 1\text{ m}^3$.
For an isochoric process, the work done $W = 0$.
According to the first law of thermodynamics, the heat involved is $Q = \Delta U + W = \Delta U$.
The change in internal energy is given by $\Delta U = n C_v \Delta T$.
Using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$, we have $n\Delta T = \frac{V\Delta P}{R}$.
Substituting this into the expression for $Q$:
$Q = C_v \left( \frac{V(P_2 - P_1)}{R} \right)$
Given values: $n = 10$ moles, $V = 1\text{ m}^3$, $P_1 = 21.7\text{ Pa}$, $P_2 = 30\text{ Pa}$, $C_v = 21\text{ J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$, and $R = 8.3\text{ J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$.
$Q = 21 \times \frac{1 \times (30 - 21.7)}{8.3}$
$Q = 21 \times \frac{8.3}{8.3} = 21\text{ J}$.
Thus, the value of $\alpha$ is 21.

q: When 300 J of heat given to an ideal gas with $C_{p}=\frac{7}{2} R$ its temperature raises from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ keeping its volume constant. If n is the number of moles of the gas, then what is the value of $100n$? $(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}. \mathrm{K})$
question: When 300 J of heat given to an ideal gas with $C_{p}=\frac{7}{2} R$ its temperature raises from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ keeping its volume constant. If n is the number of moles of the gas, then what is the value of $100n$? $(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}. \mathrm{K})$
solution:
Given: $C_P = \frac{7}{2}R$, so $C_V = C_P - R = \frac{5}{2}R$
$\Delta Q = nC_V \Delta T$ (constant volume)
$300 = n \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.314 \times 30$
$n = 0.48$ mol
$100n = 48$

q: In the Young's double slit experiment the intensity produced by each one of the individual slits is $I_{0}$. The distance between two slits is 2 mm. The distance of screen from slits is 10 m. The wavelength of light is $6000 \mathrm{~A}^{\circ}$. The intensity of light on the screen in front of one of the slits is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: In the Young's double slit experiment the intensity produced by each one of the individual slits is $I_{0}$. The distance between two slits is 2 mm. The distance of screen from slits is 10 m. The wavelength of light is $6000 \mathrm{~A}^{\circ}$. The intensity of light on the screen in front of one of the slits is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
In Young's double slit experiment:
$d = 2$ mm, $D = 10$ m, $\lambda = 6000$ Å
The point in front of one slit is at $y = \frac{d}{2}$ from the central axis.
$I = 4I_0 \cos^2\left(\frac{\pi d y}{\lambda D}\right)$
$= 4I_0 \cos^2\left(\frac{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 10}\right)$
$= 4I_0 \cos^2\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = 4I_0 \times \frac{1}{4} = I_0$

q: A point source is kept at the center of a spherically enclosed detector. If the volume of the detector increased by 8 times, the intensity will
question: A point source is kept at the center of a spherically enclosed detector. If the volume of the detector increased by 8 times, the intensity will
solution:
For a point source, intensity is given by $I = \frac{P}{4\pi r^2}$ where $P$ is power and $r$ is distance.
If the volume of the spherical detector increases by 8 times, then $\frac{4}{3}\pi r_{new}^3 = 8 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{old}^3$, giving $r_{new} = 2r_{old}$.
The new intensity is $I_{new} = \frac{P}{4\pi (2r_{old})^2} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{P}{4\pi r_{old}^2} = \frac{I_{old}}{4}$.
Therefore intensity decreases by 4 times.

q: The fifth harmonic of a closed organ pipe is found to be in unison with the first harmonic of an open pipe. The ratio of lengths of closed pipe to that of the open pipe is $5 / x$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The fifth harmonic of a closed organ pipe is found to be in unison with the first harmonic of an open pipe. The ratio of lengths of closed pipe to that of the open pipe is $5 / x$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Fifth harmonic of closed organ pipe equals first harmonic of open pipe:
$f_{5\text{ closed}} = f_{1\text{ open}}$
$\frac{5v}{4L_{\text{closed}}} = \frac{v}{2L_{\text{open}}}$
$\frac{L_{\text{closed}}}{L_{\text{open}}} = \frac{5}{2}$
Since the ratio is $\frac{5}{x}$, we get $x = 2$.

q: In case of vertical circular motion of a particle by a thread of length $r$ if the tension in the thread is zero at an angle $30^{\circ}$ shown in figure, the velocity at the bottom point $(A)$ of the circular path is ($g=$ gravitational acceleration)
question: In case of vertical circular motion of a particle by a thread of length $r$ if the tension in the thread is zero at an angle $30^{\circ}$ shown in figure, the velocity at the bottom point $(A)$ of the circular path is ($g=$ gravitational acceleration)
solution:
At the point where tension $T = 0$ and angle from vertical is $60°$ (i.e., $30°$ from horizontal):
$T + mg\cos 60° = \frac{mV^2}{\ell}$
Since $T = 0$:
$V^2 = \frac{g\ell}{2}$
Using mechanical energy conservation from bottom (A) to the point where $T = 0$:
$\frac{1}{2}mu^2 = mg(\ell + \ell\cos 60°) + \frac{1}{2}mV^2$
$u^2 = 2g\ell\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) + \frac{g\ell}{2} = 3g\ell + \frac{g\ell}{2} = \frac{7g\ell}{2}$
$u = \sqrt{\frac{7g\ell}{2}}$
